dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to 814, address redacted, for a report of breathing trouble.
Hi there, I'm Alex, with the rescue squad. I see you're not feeling well - could you start by telling me why you called 911 today?
My husband started having trouble breathing about 30 minutes ago. He's on oxygen at home.
Can you tell me your name please?
Great. What year is this?
What city are we in right now?
And tell me in your own words what happened earlier that got you calling 911.
I see. So you were short of breath. Was it worse at any point?
Have you had any prior issues with your lungs or heart?
He takes albuterol, Spiriva, Symbicort, and iron pills. Oh, and oxygen at 2 liters per minute.
On a scale of 0 to 10, where 10 is the worst pain you can imagine, how would you rate your breathing difficulty right now?
Let me confirm something about your airway. Do you feel anything blocking your ability to breathe clearly?
Do you feel the need to take deep breaths or does breathing feel okay to you?
Let me quickly feel the pulse in your neck and wrist.
Pulse is 110 and strong - very fast, but consistent.
Respirations are 24 per minute and shallow.
Blood pressure is 204 over 116. That's significantly elevated.
Oxygen saturation is at 96%.
His SPO2 is 96% at home oxygen setting of 4 liters - that's a solid baseline.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event - I'll record your GCS as 15 to reflect this.
Let me look at your neck - I notice you are using your accessory muscles to breathe. That suggests some respiratory distress.
Breathing sounds are wheezy and tight, so it seems like you're having an exacerbation of COPD.
We're going to apply some ECG patches for monitoring with four leads. That's painless, but you might feel a slight chill.
Let me recheck your respiratory rate. It remains 24 per minute.
We're going to give you some nebulized albuterol and Atrovent to help open your lungs, and increase your home O2 to 8 liters per minute. How does that sound?
I've readied the nebulizer with the medications and attached it to the oxygen mask.
You're also taking docusate, magnesium oxide, and cholecalciferol at home, along with the ferrous sulfate.
Let me take your pulse again quickly to track changes. Pulse is now 101, still fast, but slightly decreased.
Your respiratory rate has dropped slightly to 22.
Your blood pressure is now 186 over 111 - it's still high but down a bit.
Your oxygen is now 99%, which is better.
EKG rhythm is now sinus tachycardia - still fast but showing some rhythm stabilization.

The EKG showed left anterior fascicular block and abnormal left axis deviation. We also saw signs of an old myocardial infarct. Additionally, a 12-lead ECG has been obtained for more detailed cardiac information.
Let me recheck your pulse and blood pressure after a few more minutes. Pulse is now 94, and blood pressure is 159 over 86.
Oxygen saturation is 98% at this point. Also, your glucose reading has improved to 88 mg/dl.
Respirations are down to 20 per minute and marginally improved in depth.
Your initial glucose reading was unreadable. We're making a note of this for hospital staff.
As documented, you're reporting no significant pain at this time. I'll record the pain score as 0.
Based on increasing wheezing, we're going to repeat the albuterol nebulizer at 8 liters of oxygen to ensure continued relief of symptoms.
I've started an 18-gauge IV in your left arm with a saline lock for future access. It may feel slightly cold, but it's for your safety.
Upon reassessment during transport, I observed the presence of unexpected stridor - this is unusual and we'll report it to the hospital team.
Let me confirm anything new - are you feeling any pain in your chest, neck, or back?
Let me recheck your vitals one more time. Pulse is 94, respiratory rate is 24, blood pressure is 187 over 95, and oxygen saturation is 97%.
Bystander, do you have a preferred hospital for treatment?
We are preparing to move you carefully onto the stretcher. I will secure you in the Fowler's position. Is there a way to make this more comfortable for you?
He usually goes to [COMPANY_NAME].
Due to his condition and elevated blood pressure, we will transport in the cold mode for safety.

All care and documentation have been completed. Staff at [COMPANY_NAME] will be informed of his condition, including a history of stroke (CVA), MI, anemia, and the IV line in his left arm. The patient reported no pain during assessment and was alert and oriented x4 (AOx4).
I just did a quick abdominal check, but I didn't find any tenderness, masses, or odd sounds at this time.
You don't seem to have any rashes, cuts, or swelling as part of this episode. Skin is also warm and dry.
I don't see any obvious issues with urine or bowel function at the moment.
No deformities or signs of injury to your legs or back so far.
I don't see or hear any drainage, obstruction, or sunken eyes in your airway.
Let's check your EKG one more time to ensure no new changes. No major issues shown at the time.
The effect from the albuterol treatment is wearing off slightly, so I'll repeat it on the way.
When was the last time the patient had something to eat or drink?
He had been sitting on the bed resting and noticed shortness of breath develop slowly over 30 minutes.
I've turned on the 12-lead ECG for a more in-depth look at your heart activity. It won't take long.
The 12-lead showed a possible old inferior MI along with the fascicular block. You'll want the hospital team to take a closer look at this.
He has no known drug allergies.
Let me test some memory now. What did you have for breakfast today?
Let's go over your breathing difficulty once more. How would you rate your trouble breathing now?
Has your level of wheezing stayed about the same, increased, or gotten better since we started treating you?
Do you feel any pressure in your chest now or at the time you called 911?
Did anything you did today before calling EMS make your shortness of breath worse?
How long have you been having this shortness of breath?
Did the increase in oxygen help at all?
Let me double-check - is there anything else you want the hospital to know about your medical history?
Bystander, is [COMPANY_NAME] the correct facility he normally visits?
You'll be more comfortable there in the hospital. I'll make sure the stretcher is adjusted to Fowler's position correctly for transport.
Airway is currently clear and patent, no signs of obstruction.
Are you still having to breathe rapidly? Can you take a slow, deep breath for me?
Skin is slightly flushed but no cyanosis at this time.
The pulses in his wrist and at the neck are equal, strong, and felt well.
I don't see signs of a fever or abnormally cold skin, so we can rule out hypothermia.
His trachea looks centered with no signs of shift or swelling noted.
I still see the use of accessory muscles during breathing. It's a sign of mild respiratory distress.
No signs of bumps, swelling, or cuts on the head or face. Eyes are alert and not sunken or irritated.
No jugular venous distention observed. That's a good sign.
No neck pain, stiffness, or lymph nodes palpated. Remains within normal findings.
No new scars or masses in the neck or head region found.
I can tell by our communication he can hear well and respond to all verbal cues.
Let me test for sensation in your feet and hands briefly. Can you feel me touching you there?
And do you have any trouble moving your arms or legs at the moment?
No deformities or swelling found while moving and checking the limbs.
Let me test again for again for wheezing or crackles. Sounds are still mostly wheezy, as before.
No significant flaring of nostrils is observed yet.
Chest expansion remains symmetrical, though still shallow.
I think we're doing well for now, but might need another round of albuterol on cue or with signs of change.
We're also keeping the IV in place in case of sudden changes in condition.
Oxygen saturation is now 98%. We're doing well here.
Let me track the pulse again - it's at 94, respirations are 20 as we noted earlier.
All straps are fully in place to stabilize, and you are in a well-supported Fowler's position.
Transport will be in cold mode due to potential cardiac activity and control of patient condition.
I'll make sure to include all IV drugs, their dosages, times, and patient info for the hospital report.
All procedures and findings have been thoroughly charted and added to the patient's file.
The patient has a history of old MI and recent stroke, and is alert and oriented during all our interactions.
He may be more comfortable with familiar staff - can he be admitted to the ward he knows?
Everything's set up and ready. Any last things for you before we head out?
Yes, go ahead. Please make sure he's safe.
I know we're in a hurry, but we'll look after him safely the whole way to [COMPANY_NAME].
Let me check your breathing one more time. It's stable for now, but keep an eye on it in case of more wheeze return.
Vitals still look stable and oxygen good. No signs of immediate decline.
Transporting with IV, ECG leads, and oxygen on. Everything handed over and reported to hospital.
Unit 3 is en route to [COMPANY_NAME] for ED care. Time en route estimated in 18 minutes based on traffic.
Let's call ahead to the hospital and give the triage team a heads up about his condition.
All transporting actions have followed the medical respiratory distress protocol correctly.
Unit 3 is en route with one emergency patient, final destination [COMPANY_NAME], time of departure now.
Check that nothing is left behind - make sure our gear is clean before heading in.
Call now ends with full staff response and patient en route to facility for continued care.
Speed and caution are both priorities during transport to ensure safety in cold mode.
All systems are working fine. Lights, sirens, and emergency routing activated.
Running backup communication to hospital as primary line will be used for patient care.
Hospital ED is aware and readying a team for receiving an elderly male with COPD and MI history.
No trends in glucose at this time - more stable once we reach the hospital staff.
Monitoring signs during transit and continuing with time-series assessments as needed.
Patient still actively breathing, no new signs of distress added during transport.
We've stayed on top of the symptoms and have kept his distress minimized.
Initial stretcher checks and patient secure confirm readiness for en route handling.
I'll document the departure and transmission of patient info at this point.
Final words to corroboration team made at switch, transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] now.
We are committed to his care and will make sure he reaches the right team at the right time safely.
We'll make our report to the hospital on his COPD exacerbation, readings, and overall transport behavior.
Patient note complete, and electronic documentation in process as we head to the VA ED.
Transport files updated, team confirmed, process is ahead of insertion at receiving ED.
Trip is official now - prepare to make drop off at [COMPANY_NAME] ED with full medical report in hand.
Still in cold mode, all vitals within transport window, ready for ED intervention.
Document all incidents in trip report. Make communication with hospital crew clear and ready for handoff.
This case followed the respiratory distress protocol correctly as of departure reference.
All has gone as planned during transit. Patient was safely handled and maintained.
